payload.answer: Use artifact-based storage with indexing and dedup/aggregation for repeated rows; keep a raw snapshot for exact formatting/punctuation and a normalized view (schema + rows) for retrieval and querying.
payload.decision: Store as an indexed dataset artifact (or reference) rather than duplicating every repeated row; preserve the schema (columns) and store row content in a retrieval-friendly form with dedup/aggregation when identical/repeated; keep punctuation/formatting fidelity in the raw source while maintaining a normalized view for querying.

entity_resolution.reason: No specific named entities from the CSV content are provided beyond generic column/table descriptors.

payload.intent: Store and organize a large CSV table (500 rows, repeated structure) in Brain’s memory system; decide an appropriate storage policy.

memory_cards.statement: For large CSV inputs with repeated/duplicated rows, store the dataset as a single artifact with indexing/deduplication rather than creating separate memory entries for each repeated row; retain the original text/source fidelity separately for traceability.

Because the CSV appears to share columns like (id, name, value), store a normalized representation (schema + row records) for querying, while optionally keeping a raw snapshot for exact punctuation and formatting.

details: Created low-confidence storage policy and data modeling cards based only on the fixture description.

repair_options: If you provide a sample of the actual CSV rows (including punctuation and any non-identical repeated patterns), I can propose a concrete dedup/aggregation rule (e.g., key by id, or by (name,value), etc.).
If you specify Brain’s intended retrieval use-cases (search by id/name, analytics by value, etc.), I can recommend an explicit index strategy and whether to store full rows vs. derived facts.
